pinDefaults.position: Head Clinical AI Strategy - Translational Research  Location: Basel  The Head Clinical AI Strategy - Translational Research will be responsible for directing and implementing the strategic roadmap and impact of Data Science & AI efforts related to early clinical development trials. This role acts as the integrator addressing key scientific questions, data‑science methodologies, and operational execution to ensure delivery of high‑value, decision‑enabling AI solutions for Translational Medicine and Biomedical Research.  This role integrates and converts complex scientific/clinical needs into coherent, realistic, and trackable strategies that incorporate data science solutions, operational execution, KPIs, ROI assessments, and value frameworks to show how the implementation of AI can improve scientific understanding, trial design, and development decisions. About the Role  Key Responsibilities: Scientific–Technical Integration: Serve as the bridge between scientific leaders and data‑science teams to decipher and articulate key scientific questions into actionable AI use-cases. Use‑Case Delivery & Scale: Identify and shape high‑value AI use cases in early clinical development to bring forward for prioritization. Support governance and decision-making processes by preparing content, facilitating meetings, and communicating the proposals to stakeholder audiences. For use cases that are endorsed, work together with clinical teams to build an actionable delivery plan and oversee delivery and assessment in partnership with the team. Oversee development of the BR Clinical Design Lab to enable scaled and process optimized implementation of data driven and AI solutions to all clinical teams approaching tollgates and governance. Define, develop and track impact measurements and KPIs to enable quantification of value of the work, both at trial and program level. In collaboration with partners in Data & Digital, Data Science teams, and enterprise partners, actively assess the data landscape that supports clinical development AI work and enable fit-for-purpose data availability. Evaluate AI startups and technology vendors to access cutting edge methodologies and recommend strategic partnerships or collaborations that accelerate AI capability development Promote awareness, adoption, and change management for AI-driven processes and foster understanding organization-wide. Demonstrate internal and external presence that brings recognition of Novartis as a leader and innovator in Data Science and AI. Serve as a strategic thought partner to clinical leadership teams in Biomedical Research and Novartis, providing insights and advice on AI enablement, scientific value, and emerging trends.   Essential Requirements: Advanced degree (PhD, MD, MSc) in relevant scientific fields. PhD or equivalent experience in Data Science, Computer Science, or related field desirable  Excellent English required (oral & written)  Minimum 10 years of relevant experience in pharmaceutical settings combined with Data Science projects Demonstratable track record of delivering global solutions at scale in healthcare settings, with a strong preference for experience in the drug development space. Experience integrating scientific, data‑science, and operational perspectives in R&D. Experience in defining KPI and ROI frameworks Proven experience developing and implementing digital or AI strategy in a complex, matrixed organization, ideally within life sciences or pharmaceutical R&D. Strong understanding of AI/ML methodologies in biomedical contexts Up-to-date knowledge of digital, AI, and data science trends, applied to Pharma or Healthcare, including expertise in aligning internal capabilities with evolving external landscapes. Excellent stakeholder engagement, influencing, and communication skills with the ability to drive organizational change and foster collaboration. Strategic thinker with the ability to see the "big picture", translating complex strategies into concrete actions and measurable impact.

inputs.label: inherit_context
inputs.description: When true, seed the child run's context.messages from the parent's active context messages. If the pin is not connected, the node checkbox is used. Default: false.

content.description: Assistant text content (may be empty for tool-call turns).

data.description: Structured output object when a response schema is used.

items.title: Tool Call
items.description: Single tool call request: tool name + JSON-safe arguments.

call_id.description: Optional stable id for matching results (call_id).

name.description: Tool name (must exist in the runtime tool registry).

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Token usage counters (provider-dependent; fields may be missing).

inputs.label: use_context
inputs.description: When true, include this run's active context messages (context.messages) in the LLM request. If the pin is not connected, the node checkbox is used. Default: false.

inputs.label: context
inputs.description: Optional explicit context object for this call (e.g. {messages:[...]}). If provided, context.messages overrides inherited run context messages.

inputs.label: memory
inputs.description: Memory configuration object (KG/span/session controls). If set, overrides this call’s recall behavior; if unset, runtime defaults apply.

inputs.label: provider
inputs.description: Text/LLM provider id (e.g. LMStudio). If unset, uses the node’s configured provider.

inputs.label: model
inputs.description: Text/LLM model id/name. If unset, uses the node’s configured model.

inputs.label: system
inputs.description: Optional system prompt for this single call.

inputs.label: prompt
inputs.description: User prompt/content for this single call.

inputs.label: tools
inputs.description: Allowlist of tools exposed to the model as ToolSpecs (model may request tool calls; execution is done via a Tool Calls node).

inputs.label: max_in_tokens
inputs.description: Optional per-call input token budget (max_input_tokens). When set, overrides the run's default _limits.max_input_tokens for this call.

inputs.label: temperature
inputs.description: Sampling temperature (0 = deterministic). If unset, uses the node’s configured temperature.

inputs.label: seed
inputs.description: Seed for deterministic sampling (-1 = random/unset). If unset, uses the node’s configured seed.

inputs.label: thinking
inputs.description: Reasoning/thinking control for supported models. If unset, uses the Gateway/runtime default.

inputs.label: resp_schema
inputs.description: Optional JSON Schema object (type=object) the assistant content must conform to.

inputs.label: prompt_cache_binding
inputs.description: Advanced: durable exact-reuse prompt-cache binding from Gateway blocs. Accepts a binding object or binding_id string; this does not create or load blocs during the run.

outputs.label: response
outputs.description: Assistant text content (best-effort). For tool calls, content may be empty.

outputs.label: data
outputs.description: Structured assistant output object matching resp_schema. Visible by default when a response schema is configured.

outputs.label: success
outputs.description: True if the LLM call completed successfully.

outputs.label: meta
outputs.description: Host-facing meta envelope (schema=abstractflow.llm_call.v1.meta). Includes provider/model, usage, trace ids, and lightweight execution metadata.

outputs.label: tool_calls
outputs.description: Normalized tool call requests. This pin exists to make wiring into Tool Calls / Emit Event nodes simpler.

pinDefaults.prompt: Your goal is to write the EDUCATION, PUBLICATION AND SKILLS section of the resume.

pinDefaults.a:
You are a professional resume writer.

This is your authentic voice, follow it:

data.label: Authentic Voice
data.literalValue:
# Authentic Voice and Thinking Style Memory

This file describes Laurent-Philippe's authentic thinking and writing style for downstream writers and reviewers. It is not a template. It is a constraint on how to transform the factual memories into public prose.

Core thinking pattern:
- Start from the scientific, biomedical, clinical, regulatory, user, or operational problem.
- Understand what is currently hard, ambiguous, missing, or repeated.
- Identify the missing representation, data layer, semantic layer, computational method, user workflow, or review mechanism.
- Build or test that missing layer with the people facing the question.
- Use the layer to make a deeper question tractable: prediction, explanation, comparison, reuse, review, collaboration, or decision.
- AI is a method, not the goal.

Biomedical purpose:
- The goal is not to `turn biomedical questions into AI use cases`.
- The goals are answering difficult biomedical questions, finding better therapies, understanding which patients respond and why, improving drug-development and regulatory decisions, improving the pipeline, helping scientists and clinicians work better, and ultimately helping patients.
- AI/data capabilities can be described as a practical way to prioritize, analyze, compare, retrieve, predict, explain, review, or measure something that matters.

Typical authentic themes:
- Representation matters: molecular surfaces, semantic graphs, JSON-LD, FAIR data models, causal signatures, synthetic data graphs, article-first analyses, cognitive architectures.
- Context matters: molecules in cellular environment, patients in cohorts and trial arms, documents in regulatory corpora, models in orchestration loops, data in workflows.
- Review matters: provenance, assumptions, source references, traceability, human-review boundaries, testing, quality/privacy trade-offs, model behavior over time.
- Adoption matters: users, research camps, onboarding, active users, time saved, reusable workflows, data products that people can actually use.
- Prediction matters: target/anti-target prediction, side-effect prediction, repositioning, patient / trial-arm similarity, causal signatures, multimodal integration, clinical-trial optimization.

Tone:
- Direct, precise, explanatory, pragmatic, and concrete.
- Senior without sounding like a generic corporate executive.
- Scientific without sounding like a dissertation.
- Confident but not grandiose.
- Collaborative and humble: work with scientists, clinicians, engineers, regulatory experts, medical teams, commercial teams, and users.
- Avoid ego-coded separations such as `so others can use it`; use joint practice and collaboration.

Sentence shape:
- Laurent-Philippe often explains the problem, the mechanism, and the consequence in sequence.
- He may use longer explanatory sentences when the logic is important, but public resumes should avoid overloaded chains of disconnected facts.
- Strong writing should make the purpose and mechanism clear before naming frameworks or tools.
- Avoid polished generic openings that sound like an AI wrote a job application.

Good transformation pattern:
- Weak AI-style framing: `I help teams turn biomedical questions into AI use cases`.
- Better authentic framing: start from the biomedical problem, then say the AI/data layer helps compare, predict, retrieve, structure, or review what matters.
- Weak AI-style framing: `source-visible AI/ML for portfolio decisions`.
- Better authentic framing: the system keeps document references, assumptions, provenance, and review points visible so experts can use the result without losing the link to the original material.
- Weak AI-style framing: `external-method judgment`.
- Better authentic framing: built and evaluated computational methods and platforms for real pharma / biotech drug-discovery questions.

Words and phrases to avoid in public assets:
- `Roche Computational Biomedicine`.
- `turn biomedical questions into AI use cases` as a main goal.
- `source-visible AI/ML`.
- `evidence people can check`.
- `external-method judgment`.
- `failure envelopes`.
- `safety-relevant observability`.
- `autonomy boundaries` unless discussing AI architecture in a technical document.
- `operating model` when a simpler phrase works.
- `This matters because` as an explanatory bridge.
- `For [employer], this matters because` as a repeated bridge.
- `natural collaborator`.
- `names to borrow`.
- `conversation to earn`.
- Generic phrases like `AI-ready solutions`, `robust semantic layer`, `decision-enabling AI` unless immediately grounded in a concrete system, data, user, or workflow.

Preferred concrete verbs:
- built, co-led, structured, introduced, coordinated, reduced, enabled, tested, deployed, mentored, analyzed, compared, predicted, integrated, modeled, visualized, reviewed, made reusable, made inspectable.

Voice guardrails for job applications:
- Do not paste reviewer recommendations as resume text.
- Do not reuse canned sentences across applications.
- Do not stitch together existing fragments as if they were a tailored document.
- Select facts from the memory base, understand the role, and write new prose.
- Use the candidate's own logic: problem -> missing layer -> system/method -> what it enables -> evidence or ROI.
- Preserve technical substance; do not flatten into generic leadership language.
- Make ROI and adoption visible when relevant: weeks/months saved, users, onboarding reduction, public-resource scale, grants, customer work, research-camp productivity, faster analysis.

Examples of authentic factual framing:
- BIONEXT: built multimodal structural-bioinformatics and multi-omics pipelines to predict unexpected targets, anti-targets, side effects, and repositioning opportunities, reducing target-identification cycles from months to computational analysis.
- RAGnarok: structures large scientific and regulatory corpora into entities, relationships, document references, provenance, and reusable evidence objects so experts can query eCTD, EPAR, reimbursement, and scientific corpora without losing the link to original material.
- Digital Article: helps technical and non-technical experts explore data while preserving the scientific question, executable code, methods, results, and structured outputs.
- FAIR 2.0: helps data-structuration efforts because good AI needs a strong data foundation, not only a model added on top of poorly structured data.
- Synthetic data: introduces QPS to reason about the necessary balance between preserved data quality and privacy risks.

Reviewer obligations:
- Review for factual accuracy, not only style.
- Check whether the output preserved prediction, multimodal integration, ROI, adoption, scientific purpose, and user-facing value when the role calls for them.
- Flag invented organization names, missing ROI, missing Apollo/TRACE, missing AI Champion when relevant, missing BVS for drug-discovery roles, and missing FAIR/Semantic Data Layer for data-foundation roles.
- If the voice feels AI-written, the reviewer must give concrete edits tied to this memory, not vague criticism.

Provenance class:
- User feedback across application work, original articles, thesis style, public Medium writing, resumes, and manually rewritten examples provided by Laurent-Philippe.
